title: Frustrated superradiant phases in one- and two-dimensional lattices
abstract: Understanding how frustration and symmetry breaking shape collective behavior is a central problem in quantum many-body systems. In this work, we investigate this problem in large one- and two-dimensional arrays of coupled Dicke models on a periodic lattice, where strong light-matter coupling gives rise to a superradiant phase and competition between neighboring order parameters induces spontaneous translational symmetry breaking. Such Dicke lattice models constitute a fundamentally new class of quantum many-body systems, as they simultaneously realize the thermodynamic limit associated with the lattice size and an intrinsic thermodynamic limit arising from collective on-site interactions with quantum emitters. We show that frustration drives photonic density-wave ordering, and that the resulting broken periodicity can be predicted from the excitation spectrum of the symmetric phase, without requiring computationally prohibitive thermodynamic energy minimization. Furthermore, we demonstrate that an emergent Nambu-Goldstone mode arises near the critical point in a one-dimensional chain despite the presence of only discrete symmetry, and uncover the mechanism that enables this otherwise forbidden gapless excitation. We also find quasi-periodic ordering in the superradiant phase, reminiscent of quasicrystals, and demonstrate that synthetic magnetic flux provides a powerful knob to control the nature of translational symmetry breaking. Our results establish a new direction in quantum many-body physics where the coexistence of local and global thermodynamic limits gives rise to unconventional symmetry breaking and emergent collective behavior.
summary: The paper investigates superradiant phase transitions in frustrated Dicke lattice models. It demonstrates that geometric frustration leads to density-wave ordering and predicts the emergence of a Nambu-Goldstone mode near criticality by analyzing the excitation spectrum. This establishes a new theoretical framework for understanding symmetry breaking in coupled quantum emitter arrays.
main_problem: The central problem is understanding how geometric frustration and symmetry breaking govern collective behavior in quantum many-body systems, specifically focusing on superradiant phase transitions.
main_result: Frustration drives photonic density-wave ordering, leading to emergent Nambu-Goldstone modes near criticality, which can be predicted by analyzing the excitation spectrum rather than energy minimization.
method: The work employs mean-field theory combined with a detailed analysis of quantum fluctuations around the mean-field solution, extending techniques from lattice Hamiltonians and Ginzburg-Landau frameworks.
model_or_system: The primary model is arrays of coupled Dicke models on periodic lattices (1D and 2D). The Hamiltonian includes local light-matter coupling terms ($\lambda$) and hopping interactions ($J$), allowing for the study of superradiance in engineered quantum optical systems.
key_observables: Superradiant phase transition point, photonic density-wave ordering, Nambu-Goldstone mode emergence (gapless excitation), quasi-periodic order, and critical exponents ($\gamma_F$).
important_parameters: Strong light-matter coupling regime; lattice size parity ($N$ even vs. odd); synthetic magnetic flux ($\phi_{r,r'}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on approximating the system by analyzing the excitation spectrum of the symmetric phase to bypass computationally prohibitive energy minimization.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the model setup (Dicke lattice), show order parameter evolution across different coupling strengths ($\lambda$), and plot the excitation energy ($\epsilon$) revealing both standard mean-field scaling and anomalous, size-dependent critical exponents for odd $N$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the general Dicke lattice model to analyzing the 1D chain case by examining parity dependence (even vs. odd N), deriving the anomalous critical exponent, and finally generalizing the findings to show the emergence of a true Nambu-Goldstone mode in the thermodynamic limit.
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum optics (Dicke model) with fundamental condensed matter concepts like frustration and symmetry breaking, providing concrete theoretical predictions for emergent gapless modes in engineered light-matter arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the Dicke lattice model. An example lattice that consists of six sites. Each node at ri represents a Dicke model where a single cavity mode ari is coupled to the collective spin Jri. The blue dotted arrow de- notes the connectivity. hi,j denotes the hopping Hamiltonian Hhop ri,rj, which describes the photon hopping between site ri and site rj with the strength Jri,rj and the phase ϕri,rj.

FIG. 2. Schematic illustration and numerical results for the simple Dicke chain model. (a) A schematic of the model con- sisting of N sites with the periodic boundary condition. Each red circle represents the Dicke model with a macroscopic num- ber of spins and a single cavity mode. (b) The order parameter ¯αn at site n is plotted as a function of the coupling strength λ, with λc denoting the critical coupling strength at which the superradiant phase transition occurs. The insets illustrate the spatial distribution in the superradiant phase in real space. (c) The excitation energy ϵ is shown along the coupling strength λ, and a low-lying state of N = 9 is highlighted in the purple box at a...

FIG. 3. Numerical results for the simple Dicke chain model. (a) Excitation energy ϵk as a function of momentum k for various coupling strengths in the normal phase, evaluated for a large system size. (b) Comparison of the order parameter across different system sizes. Black dots represent the amplitude difference between nearest neighbors, while red dots indicate the ratio between the maximum and minimum amplitudes of the order parameter, αmax and αmin. (c) Order parameter and excitation energy as functions of the coupling strength for a chain of 71 sites. For selected coupling strengths marked by the dashed red lines, the real-space distribution of the order parameter is shown. (d)...

FIG. 4. Schematics and numerical results for the infinite-sized Dicke triangular lattice model are shown. Schematic illustrations show the Dicke triangular lattice model in (a) the normal phase and (b) the superradiant phase, with circle size and color indicating the amplitude and sign of the order parameter, respectively. In (a), the gray dashed line and black arrows represent the minimal unit cell and the lattice vector of the triangular lattice. In (b), the gray dashed line and black arrows denote the extended unit cell, which includes three sublattices, along with the lattice vector. The excitation energy band is shown relative to the critical coupling strength, using (c) the minimal...

FIG. 5. Schematic and numerical results for the Dicke ladder model. (a) Three equivalent representations of the Dicke ladder model, where each gray circle corresponds to a Dicke model. The black arrow indicates the gauge field direction of the magnetic flux. (b) Excitation energy ϵk plotted in momentum space for various coupling strengths λ. (c) Schematic illustrations showing cases where the order parameter configuration is commensurate (top) and incommensurate (bottom) with the lattice structure.

Partially Pumped Collective Emitters.note: The new paper studies superradiant phase transitions in coupled Dicke lattice models, which directly relates to the collective emission physics and symmetry breaking expected when studying engineered arrays of emitters.
